---
title: "Validation"
subtitle: "Closed-form vs. Monte Carlo cross-check, plus the tail metrics the closed form cannot give"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

::: {.side-panel-right}
```{=html}
<aside class="side-panel scorecard-sidebar">
<details open>
<summary>Reading the validation plots</summary>
<dl>
  <dt><code>z = (MC − cf) / stderr</code></dt>
  <dd><code>|z| ≤ 2</code> is sampling noise; <code>|z| &gt; 3</code>
  points at a simulator bug.</dd>

  <dt><code>±CI<sub>95</sub> = 1.96 · SD / √n</code></dt>
  <dd>95% half-width of the MC mean. Shrinks as
  <code>1 / √n<sub>paths</sub></code>.</dd>

  <dt>Variance ratio</dt>
  <dd>Fee and back-to-back satisfy
  <code>Var[Π<sub>b2b</sub>] / Var[R<sub>fee</sub>] = (P / f)²</code>
  — algebraic, not statistical. Any MC disagreement is a bug.</dd>

  <dt>Partial / syndicated SD scaling</dt>
  <dd><code>SD[Π<sub>α,β</sub>] = (1 − α)(1 − β) · SD[Π<sub>b2b</sub>]</code>.
  The expected signature of every sweep plot on the page.</dd>

  <dt>VaR / CVaR on the fee book</dt>
  <dd>Read as "low end of revenue", not loss — fee revenue is
  <code>≥ 0</code> by construction.</dd>

  <dt>Merton row</dt>
  <dd>Expect <code>|z| ≲ 2</code> against the GBM closed form
  (compensation is firing) and SD (merton) strictly above SD (gbm).
  Flat SD means the overlay is inert.</dd>

  <dt>Switching row</dt>
  <dd>Closed-form columns are <code>NaN</code> — no closed-form
  density. Compare MC tails directly to the no-switch anchor.</dd>
</dl>
</details>
</aside>
```
:::

The closed-form identities on the [Model](phase-a.qmd) page are the
anchors of this page, and the simulator is the instrument. Each
section below takes one identity — a mean, a variance ratio, a
scaling law, the Merton compensation — and checks the Monte Carlo
against it. Tail metrics that have no closed form (VaR, CVaR, loss
probability) are reported alongside so the comparison lands in the
same view. All computation runs in TypeScript under `src/` and emits
the JSON this page reads; regenerate with `npm run simulate -- --seed
42 && npm run sweep -- --seed 42`.

The sidebar spells out the scorecard conventions in full; in short,
the $z$ column is the primary sanity check ($|z| \le 2$ healthy,
$|z| > 3$ points at a bug), and the variance ratio $(P/f)^2$ between
back-to-back and fee is algebraic — any disagreement there is a bug,
not a sampling artifact. P&L is the horizon total in USD; divide by
$N = \lambda T$ for a per-tonne figure.

## One parameter setting, full distributions

This block fixes all parameters and shows the full P&L distribution
of every book. The [sweep below](#parameter-sweep) is the
complementary view — one book, one risk statistic, parameters sweep.
Data is loaded from `data/run-42.json`.


```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

### Run parameters

$(\mu, \sigma, T)$ set the price process, $\lambda$ the throughput,
$(f, Q, \alpha)$ the business levers. The break-even quote $Q^*$ is
derived from the others and is the point at which the fee and
back-to-back means coincide (their distributions still differ — see
[direct comparison](phase-a.qmd#direct-comparison)).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (hedge ratio)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### Scorecard: closed form vs Monte Carlo

One row per book — fee, matched, back-to-back, partial, syndicated
retained, and switching when a barrier is active. Left block:
closed-form moments versus their MC estimates, with the $z$ column
checking their agreement. Right block: tail metrics that have no
closed form. The switching row prints `NaN` in the closed-form
columns (no closed-form moments) but its MC tails are directly
comparable.

<div class="legend-table">

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (cf) / (mc) | closed-form and Monte Carlo mean |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | 95% half-width of the MC mean, $1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD} / \sqrt{n}$ |
| SD (cf) / (mc) | closed-form and MC standard deviation |
| $z$ | $(\mathrm{MC} - \mathrm{cf}) / \mathrm{stderr}$ — primary sanity check |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | loss thresholds at 5% / 1% |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | tail-mean losses inside those thresholds |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ | probability of a losing realisation |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$ — $f$-invariant on the fee book |

</div>

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### Merton jumps: means invariant, variances inflated

The [compensated Merton overlay](phase-a.qmd#compensated-merton-jump-diffusion)
has two signatures. Means should be identical to pure GBM — the
compensation $-\lambda_J \kappa$ with $\kappa = e^{\mu_J + \sigma_J^2
/ 2} - 1$ exists precisely to preserve $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu
t}$. Variances should not — jumps genuinely fatten the tail. The
table below checks both at a canonical overlay ($\lambda_J = 3$/yr,
$\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$) by comparing the overlay's MC
moments against the GBM closed form. A healthy row satisfies $|z|
\lesssim 2$ (means agree) and **SD (merton)** strictly above **SD
(gbm-cf)** (jumps firing). Persistent large $|z|$ points at broken
compensation; flat SD points at an inert overlay.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.jumpCheck.rows, {
  columns: ["name","gbmClosedMean","mertonMcMean","mertonMcCi95","gbmClosedSd","mertonMcSd","zVsGbmClosed"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    gbmClosedMean: "E[Π] gbm-cf",
    mertonMcMean: "E[Π] merton-mc",
    mertonMcCi95: "±CI95",
    gbmClosedSd: "SD gbm-cf",
    mertonMcSd: "SD merton-mc",
    zVsGbmClosed: "z vs gbm-cf"
  },
  format: {
    gbmClosedMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    gbmClosedSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mertonMcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zVsGbmClosed: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
  }
})
```

_Overlay set by the CLI's `JUMP_CHECK` constant. Matching means
is the compensation identity $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$
firing; the widened SD column is the jumps landing._

Spot-check on the same run, at the terminal spot:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] gbm closed</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.gbmClosed.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] merton mc</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.mertonMcMean.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">z vs gbm closed</div><div class="value">${(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed).toFixed(2)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### P&L distributions, one panel per stochastic book

Terminal P&L on the x-axis; solid black rule is the closed-form mean
and the dashed grey rule is $\Pi = 0$. Mass to the left of the grey
rule is the loss probability $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ reported in the
scorecard. The fee panel is non-negative and right-skewed (it
inherits $I_T$'s right skew). The back-to-back panel is the mirror
image — same kernel with opposite sign, upside capped at $Q N$,
downside unbounded. The partial panel at $\alpha = 0.5$ is that same
mirror narrowed by $(1 - \alpha) = 0.5$ on the SD scale — the visible
fingerprint of the partial variant's $(1-\alpha)^2$ variance collapse.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// 3a is deterministic (spike at its closed-form mean), so it's omitted from the
// per-stochastic-book panel. 3e only appears when the fixture was generated
// with a finite barrier (see src/report.ts:248-250).
pnlModels = [
  "fee", "principal_3b", "principal_3c", "principal_3d",
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switching ? ["principal_3e"] : []),
]

pnlData = [
  ...run.sampleTraces.fee.map(v => ({model: "fee", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.b2b.map(v => ({model: "principal_3b", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.partial.map(v => ({model: "principal_3c", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.retained.map(v => ({model: "principal_3d", pnl: v})),
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switching
    ? run.sampleTraces.switching.map(v => ({model: "principal_3e", pnl: v}))
    : []),
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L Π  (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  fy: { label: "model" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: pnlModels, label: "model" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlData, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "model", fy: "model", thresholds: 60 })),
    // Switching has no closed-form mean (NaN) — fall back to MC mean so the
    // overlay is still meaningful on that facet.
    Plot.ruleX(
      run.rows
        .filter(r => pnlModels.includes(r.name))
        .map(r => ({ name: r.name, x: Number.isFinite(r.closedFormMean) ? r.closedFormMean : r.mcMean })),
      { x: "x", fy: "name", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }
    )
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey: $\Pi = 0$._

### $I_T$ itself — the kernel behind everything

Fee, back-to-back and partial are all linear (or affine) in
$I_T = \int_0^T S_t \, dt$; any discrepancy between their
closed-form and MC means in the scorecard traces back to whether
$I_T$ itself is being sampled correctly. Dufresne (2001) gives mean
and variance in closed form, plotted as rules on top of the MC
histogram below. A converged MC puts about 68% of the mass between
the dashed rules; tail quantiles have no closed form (since $I_T$ is
not log-normal) and are left to Monte Carlo.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$. Dashed: $\pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$._

### The matched book's interim NAV

Terminal P&L on the matched book is deterministic, but mark-to-market
value is not. $V_t = N P (1 - t/T) \cdot S_t$ — remaining inventory
times spot — is a scaled GBM that pins to zero at $T$ (the burn
schedule drains the inventory) but can carve out a material interim
dip on a mid-horizon crash. The sampled trajectories below (orange
first path, grey the rest) show the qualitative shape before the
shortfall plot formalises it.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Grey: sampled NAV paths. Orange: the first path, highlighted. $V_T$
pins to zero regardless of $S_T$._

### Shortfall-against-schedule on the matched book

The operational question on the matched book is not "what is
terminal P&L" (it's deterministic) but *how far below the flat-price
schedule did NAV dip at the worst moment*. Formally,

$$
D := \max_{t \le T} \bigl[ N P (1 - t/T) (S_0 - S_t) \bigr]_+
$$

— paper gains do not count, only below-schedule excursions. Dashed
black marks the 95th percentile (≈ 1 year in 20), solid black the
99th (≈ 1 year in 100). Deterministic terminal P&L does not make the
matched book risk-free.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed black: 95th-percentile shortfall. Solid black: 99th
percentile. The natural solvency thresholds even though terminal
P&L is deterministic._

## Parameter sweep {#parameter-sweep}

Inverting the single-run view: fix the book (partial) and the risk
statistic (CVaR₉₅, larger = worse), and let the business parameters
$(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ sweep. The two plots that follow peel off one
axis each. Data from `data/sweep.json`; slice with the controls.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "hedge ratio α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: 0.05, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility $\sigma$

More volatility widens $\mathrm{Var}[I_T]$ and therefore the
principal book's loss tail. The curve below should rise monotonically
in $\sigma$ for every $(\alpha, \mu)$; a flat or non-monotone line is
a bug.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (partial)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Partial-book CVaR₉₅ against $\sigma$ at the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$._

### Tail risk vs coverage $\alpha$

Dose-response for pre-buying, at the middle $\sigma$ grid value. The
partial variant's $(1 - \alpha)$ SD scaling law predicts CVaR
collapses roughly linearly toward zero as $\alpha$ sweeps from 0
(back-to-back) to 1 (matched). At $\alpha = 1$ the book is
deterministic, so CVaR reduces to the sign of the matched-book P&L —
a single value, not a tail.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Coverage α vs tail risk on the partial book. σ fixed at the second grid value. At α = 1 the book is deterministic so CVaR95 collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "hedge ratio α   (0 = back-to-back, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (partial)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## Syndicated cession and the $(1-\beta)^2$ law

The three plots in this section exercise the syndicated book. See
[the syndicated book in the model](phase-a.qmd#syndicated-book) for
the formulae. The key identity: at the fair premium ($\theta = 0$),
$\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}} = \mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha] + (1 - \alpha)(1 -
\beta) \cdot (\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} - \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}])$
— an affine rescaling of $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$ around
$\mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha]$, slope $(1 - \alpha)(1 - \beta)$. Every
plot below is derived from the sweep cells' back-to-back statistics;
the closed-form map means **no new Monte Carlo is needed** to price
retention. The `cvar`-mode shape factor $\phi(\Phi^{-1}(0.95))/0.05
\approx 2.063$ keeps `sharpe` and `cvar` loads on a common SD scale.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickSigma = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.sigmas, { value: sweep.grid.sigmas[1], label: "volatility σ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickTheta = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.05, value: 0, label: "counterparty load θ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMode = Inputs.radio(["sharpe", "cvar"], { value: "sharpe", label: "premium mode" })
```

### Retained CVaR₉₅ across $(\alpha, \beta)$

At the selected $(\mu, \sigma, \theta)$, each curve below is one
$\alpha$ from the sweep grid as $\beta$ sweeps 0 → 1. The retained
tail collapses linearly in $(1 - \beta)$ for every $\alpha$; the two
dials multiply into $(1 - \alpha)(1 - \beta)$ on the SD axis. Top
curve: $\alpha = 0$ (back-to-back, no internal hedge). Bottom:
$\alpha = 1$ (matched, deterministic before cession). Raising
$\theta$ lifts every curve by the counterparty risk premium
$\beta (1 - \alpha) \rho(\theta)$ — the intermediary pays mean in
exchange for the tighter tail.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
gaussianCvar95 = 2.062713055949736
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
betaGrid = Array.from({ length: 21 }, (_, i) => i / 20)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
retainedCvarCurves = {
  // E[Π_α] = α · matched + (1-α) · E[Π_b2b]; at θ=0 the cession is mean-preserving.
  // CVaR95(Π_ret) = (1-α)(1-β)·cvar95(b2b) − (1-α)·β·mean(b2b) − α·matched
  //                 + β(1-α)·loadFactor·sd(b2b)·θ   (θ load is an extra loss to the
  //                 intermediary and therefore adds to -E[Π_ret]).
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const rows = [];
  for (const alpha of sweep.grid.alphas) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const cell = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === alpha && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
      if (!cell) continue;
      const b2bMean = cell.b2b.mean;
      const b2bCvar = cell.b2b.cvar95;
      const b2bSd = cell.b2b.sd;
      const matchedMean = cell.matched.mean;
      // Identity derived from Π_ret = E[Π_α] + (1-α)(1-β)·(Π_b2b − E[Π_b2b]) + (π_loaded − π_fair).
      // The last term is −β(1-α)·θ·loadFactor·sd(b2b); it translates the distribution.
      const fairCvar = (1 - alpha) * (1 - beta) * b2bCvar
                      - (1 - alpha) * beta * b2bMean
                      - alpha * matchedMean;
      const loadShift = beta * (1 - alpha) * pickTheta * loadFactor * b2bSd;
      rows.push({ alpha, beta, cvar: fairCvar + loadShift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Retained CVaR₉₅ at the selected (μ, σ, θ). Each line is one α from the sweep grid; β sweeps 0→1. The collapse is linear in (1-β) for every α, and θ > 0 shifts curves upward (more tail loss) because the intermediary pays away mean.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π_ret   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "hedge ratio α", domain: sweep.grid.alphas, scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(retainedCvarCurves, { x: "beta", y: "cvar", stroke: "alpha", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Fair vs loaded premium

At $\alpha = 0$ the fair premium $\pi_{\mathrm{fair}} = \beta \cdot
\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ is linear in $\beta$ (the blue
$\theta = 0$ line below). A positive $\theta$ carves the loaded
premium downward by $\beta \rho(\theta)$; the vertical gap between
$\theta = 0$ and any other curve is exactly the risk premium the
counterparty extracts for absorbing tail risk.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
premiumCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const piFair = beta * cell0.b2b.mean;
      const piLoaded = piFair - beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, pi: piLoaded, isFair: theta === 0 });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Premium paid to the intermediary as a function of β, for a handful of θ values. θ = 0 is the actuarially fair premium (the straight blue line). Larger θ carves the curve down — the intermediary surrenders expected P&L to tighten the retained tail.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "premium π   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(premiumCurves, { x: "beta", y: "pi", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Expected-P&L cost of syndication

The flip side of the previous plot. At $\theta = 0$, expected
retained P&L is independent of $\beta$ — the no-free-lunch
identity — and the curve below is flat at zero. For $\theta > 0$
the shortfall from the fair premium shows up directly as a linear
drag on $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}] - \mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha]$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
meanShiftCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const shift = -beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, shift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected-P&L cost of syndication as a function of β, at α = 0. The θ = 0 curve is the flat no-free-lunch line at zero; larger θ carves linearly downward.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "E[Π_ret] − E[Π_α]   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(meanShiftCurves, { x: "beta", y: "shift", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

## Switching and tail truncation

Partial and syndicated **rescale** the loss tail by $(1 - \alpha)(1 -
\beta)$; the switching book **truncates** it, flipping the stochastic
leg to a fee book at rate $f_{\mathrm{post}}$ the first time $S_t$
crosses $H = h S_0$ (stopping time $\tau$). See [the switching book
in the model](phase-a.qmd#switching-book) for the formulae. No
closed-form density exists — $\tau$ is a stopping time — so Monte
Carlo is authoritative. Under pure GBM the Harrison (1985)
first-passage distribution gives $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ and
$\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$ as test oracles; Merton jumps inflate
$\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ and shorten $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingSweep = FileAttachment("data/switching-sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingRows = switchingSweep.cells.map(c => ({
  // h = Infinity is serialised as null — label it for the x-axis.
  h: c.h === null ? Infinity : c.h,
  hLabel: c.h === null ? "∞ (no switch)" : c.h.toFixed(2),
  mean: c.row.mean,
  cvar95: c.row.cvar95,
  cvar99: c.row.cvar99,
  probLoss: c.row.probLoss,
  probSwitch: c.switching ? c.switching.probSwitch.mc : 0,
  expectedTau: c.switching ? c.switching.expectedTau.mc : switchingSweep.base.T,
  meanFracInFeeMode: c.switching ? c.switching.meanFracInFeeMode : 0,
  retainedMean: c.retained.mean,
  retainedCvar95: c.retained.cvar95,
  feeMean: c.fee.mean,
  feeCvar95: c.fee.cvar95
}))
```

### CVaR₉₅ and $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ as functions of the barrier

The headline operator-decision chart. Lowering $h$ collapses
CVaR₉₅ from the syndicated reference (dashed horizontal) toward the
fee book's lightly negative CVaR and simultaneously lifts
$\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ as losses are converted to fee revenue. The knee
where both curves diverge from the no-switch baseline is the
operator's natural decision point.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 60,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Switching book — CVaR₉₅ (USD loss, lower is better) as a function of barrier h. Finite h on a log scale; the dashed horizontal is the no-switch syndicated anchor. Lower h truncates more of the loss tail but also caps more of the upside by handing flow to the fee book earlier.",
  x: { label: "barrier h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (switching)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", stroke: "#c33", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", fill: "#c33", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? 0, t: "syndicated retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected P&L across barriers. Tighter h shifts mean upward because the fee book's positive-revenue payoff replaces the back-to-back book's mean-zero-but-fat-tailed payoff on switched paths.",
  x: { label: "barrier h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "E[Π] (switching)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", stroke: "#2a7", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", fill: "#2a7", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? 0, t: "syndicated retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

### How often, and how early, the barrier fires

Two diagnostics on barrier exercise: empirical switch probability
$\mathbb{P}[\tau < T]$ and the horizon fraction spent in fee mode
$\mathbb{E}[(T - \tau) / T]$. Both drop as $h$ rises and both are
zero at $h = \infty$ by definition.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h) || true),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "probSwitch", "expectedTau", "meanFracInFeeMode", "mean", "cvar95"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      probSwitch: "P[τ<T]",
      expectedTau: "E[τ∧T]",
      meanFracInFeeMode: "E[(T−τ)/T]",
      mean: "E[Π]",
      cvar95: "CVaR95"
    },
    format: {
      probSwitch: x => x.toFixed(3),
      expectedTau: x => x.toFixed(3),
      meanFracInFeeMode: x => x.toFixed(3),
      mean: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
      cvar95: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2)
    }
  }
)
```

## The break-even quote $Q^*$ as a drift-and-horizon surface

Normalising the break-even quote by today's fee-book quote,

$$
\frac{Q^*}{(1 + f) \cdot P \cdot S_0} = \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T},
$$

gives a dimensionless premium (>1, principal must charge extra to
compensate for expected appreciation) or discount (<1, expected
cheapening lets the principal quote below the fee-book price).
Dashed rule at 1.0 is the $\mu = 0$ limit; longer horizons push every
non-zero curve further from that line.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_One curve per drift $\mu$ on the grid. The $\mu = 0$ curve sits on
the dashed unit line; positive drift sits above, negative below._
